questions:
In the context of the U.S. Federal Judiciary, distinct operational frameworks govern the appellate review process and the supervision of individuals. Using the official court structure and evidence-based practices described, identify the specific tier of courts tasked with reviewing whether the law was applied correctly in trial courts, as well as the specific behavioral model used by officers to guide supervision and reduce recidivism.

For the identified court tier, detail the following: the number of regional circuits it is divided into; the name and specific subject-matter jurisdiction of the 13th court in this tier; the standard number of judges on a deciding panel; and the titles of the three periodic statistical reports used to analyze its workload.

For the supervision model, provide: the full name of the model; a brief explanation of its three core principles; and the specific names/acronyms of the scientific assessment tools used for pretrial and post-conviction stages, respectively.

rounds.question:
In the context of the U.S. Federal Judiciary, distinct operational frameworks govern the appellate review process and the supervision of individuals. Using the official court structure and evidence-based practices described, identify the specific tier of courts tasked with reviewing whether the law was applied correctly in trial courts, as well as the specific behavioral model used by officers to guide supervision and reduce recidivism.

For the identified court tier, detail the following: the number of regional circuits it is divided into; the name and specific subject-matter jurisdiction of the 13th court in this tier; the standard number of judges on a deciding panel; and the titles of the three periodic statistical reports used to analyze its workload.

For the supervision model, provide: the full name of the model; a brief explanation of its three core principles; and the specific names/acronyms of the scientific assessment tools used for pretrial and post-conviction stages, respectively.
checklist_width: 13th Court Name: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit
13th Court Jurisdiction: Nationwide jurisdiction for specialized cases (patents, U.S. Court of International Trade, U.S. Court of Federal Claims)
Statistical Report 2: Federal Judicial Caseload Statistics
Statistical Report 3: Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary
Model Principle 1: Risk Principle (focus resources on higher risk individuals)
Model Principle 2: Need Principle (tailor interventions to criminality factors)
Model Principle 3: Responsivity Principle (reduce barriers to success)
Post-Conviction Tool: Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)
checklist_depth: Entity 1 Identity: U.S. Courts of Appeals (Logic: Sits below Supreme Court, reviews district court/law application)
Entity 2 Identity: Risk-Need-Responsivity Model (Logic: Evidence-based model for federal supervision/recidivism reduction)
rounds.rationale: The question utilizes Deep Logic by describing the 'Courts of Appeals' and the 'Risk-Need-Responsivity Model' solely through their functional roles and hierarchical positions within the provided text, without naming them upfront. It achieves Wide Aggregation by compelling the agent to retrieve granular details (jurisdictions, report titles, specific assessment acronyms) that are scattered across two distinct sections of the source material (Court Structure vs. Probation Practices).
source_nodes: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Evidence-Based Practices Probation and pretrial services officers are committed to using evidence-based practices (EBP) to promote public safety and positively impact the live
rounds.answer_a:
The **U.S. Courts of Appeals** (intermediate appellate courts) review whether the law was applied correctly in federal trial courts (district courts).[1][3][4] Officers in the federal judiciary, particularly probation and pretrial services, use the **Evidence-Based Practices Supervision Model** to guide supervision and reduce recidivism.

The Courts of Appeals comprise **12 regional circuits** plus the nationwide **U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit** (the 13th), which has exclusive subject-matter jurisdiction over patents, customs, claims against the U.S., and certain agency appeals.[1][2][3] Deciding panels typically consist of **three judges**.[1][4]

The courts issue three periodic statistical reports analyzing workload: **Appeals Commenced**, **Appeals Terminated on the Merits**, and **Pending Appeals**.[1]

The Evidence-Based Practices model rests on three core principles: (1) assess risk and needs using validated tools; (2) apply targeted interventions based on criminogenic factors; (3) promote effective supervision strategies like incentives and graduated responses. Scientific assessment tools are the **Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRAS)** for pretrial and **Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)** for post-conviction stages.

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] https://www.everycrsreport.com/reports/R48846.html  
[2] https://www.ncjw.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/07/Circuit-Court-Map.pdf  
[3] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_courts_of_appeals  
[4] https://www.justice.gov/usao/justice-101/federal-courts  
 U.S. Courts Administrative Office, Evidence-Based Practices (uscourts.gov)  
 Federal Probation Journal, Vol. 78 (2024)  
 U.S. Courts Pretrial Services Resource Guide  
 U.S. Courts Probation and Pretrial Services PCRA Manual
rounds.answer_b:
The U.S. Courts of Appeals (Circuit Courts) are the tier tasked with reviewing whether the law was applied correctly in trial courts. This tier is divided into 12 regional circuits. The 13th court is the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit, with nationwide subject-matter jurisdiction over specialized cases (e.g., patents, international trade, federal claims) [1][2]. A deciding panel typically consists of three judges. The three key periodic statistical reports are the *Judicial Business of the United States* report, the *Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary* report, and the *Federal Court Management Statistics* report [3].

The supervision model is the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model. Its three core principles are: 1) **Risk**: Match supervision intensity to an individual's risk of reoffending; 2) **Need**: Target dynamic, changeable criminogenic needs (e.g., antisocial attitudes); 3) **Responsivity**: Use cognitive-behavioral interventions tailored to an individual’s learning style [4]. For the pretrial stage, the primary scientific assessment tool is the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA), often implemented as the Public Safety Assessment (PSA) [4][5]. For the post-conviction stage, a common tool is the Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) [4].

## References
[1] United States Courts. *Court Role and Structure*. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure
[2] United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. *About the Court*. Retrieved from https://cafc.uscourts.gov/about-the-court/
[3] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. *Judicial Facts and Figures* and *Statistical Reports*. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports
[4] National Institute of Corrections. *Risk, Need, Responsivity (RNR)*. Retrieved from https://nicic.gov/key-issues/risk-need-responsivity-rnr
[5] Arnold Ventures. *Public Safety Assessment*. Retrieved from https://craftmediabucket.s3.amazonaws.com/uploads/PDFs/PSA-One-Pager.pdf
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B followed all instructions perfectly, specifically correctly identifying the titles of the statistical reports (e.g., 'Judicial Business') and the specific behavioral model (RNR) with its named principles (Risk, Need, Responsivity). Agent A failed on these specific details: it listed data metrics (e.g., 'Appeals Commenced') instead of report titles, and it provided a generic description of Evidence-Based Practices rather than the specific RNR model requested by the logic of the prompt and checklist.
